Summary: This study developed a combined model incorporating radiomics and CT imaging features to differentiate between malignant and benign subcentimeter solid pulmonary nodules (SSPNs). The model, which was trained and tested on a retrospective dataset of 324 patients, demonstrated excellent discriminative ability, potentially aiding radiologists in diagnosing SSPNs.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria are based on nodule size, confirmation of diagnosis, and image quality.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is well-defined, with malignant nodules confirmed by pathology and benign nodules confirmed by follow-up or pathology. The follow-up period of 2 years for benign nodules is reasonable.

Item4.reason: The study is a single-center retrospective analysis, with data collected from one hospital.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is contrast-enhanced CT, a standard imaging technique for pulmonary nodules. The study mentions using standard reconstruction algorithms.

Item6.reason: The study reports the CT acquisition parameters, including tube voltage, tube current, helical pitch, rotation time, and slice thickness, enhancing the replicability of the method.

Item7.reason: The study mentions the median interval time between CT scans and surgery (16 days), which is appropriate for validating the presence or absence of target conditions at the moment of imaging.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual delineation of the regions of interest (ROI) of the nodules on CT images, which constitutes segmentation.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by a radiologist, so it is not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, mentioning that a blinded radiologist manually delineated the ROIs slice by slice, avoiding adjacent vessels, bronchi, and normal lung tissue.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation in this study was performed by a single radiologist, which reflects clinical practice.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomics features, including first-order, shape, and texture features (GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, GLDM, NGTDM), which are hand-crafted features.

Item11.reason: The study mentions normalization and resampling during image pre-processing with a fixed bin width of 25, and all images were resampled to [1,1,0] after B-spline interpolation. This indicates appropriate use of image preprocessing techniques.

Item12.reason: The study used the Deepwise Multimodal Research Platform version 2.0.1.4, which is an integrated machine learning platform for medical data analysis based on the mature python Pyradiomics (version 3.0.1) and Scikit-learn (version 0.22) packages.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order, shape, and texture features) and mentions the use of a three-level naming method for features. The specific calculation formula for each feature can be found in the Pyradiomics web page (https://pyradiomics.readthedocs.io/).

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of extracted radiomic features and clinical/CT characteristics organized for analysis.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it uses hand-crafted radiomic features and logistic regression.

Item14.reason: The study assesses the interobserver reproducibility of the segmentation using ICCs and only includes features with ICC values > 0.75 in subsequent analysis, indicating removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study mentions removing less significant features when the linear correlation coefficient between any two features was greater than 0.9, indicating removal of redundant features. The feature selection method used in this study was the analysis of variance F-test.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size or provide statistical justification for the number of instances and features used.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study randomly divides the original dataset into training and validation cohorts in a 7:3 ratio and uses fivefold cross-validation, indicating a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address or analyze potential confounding factors such as scanner type or patient characteristics.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value for the classification task. ROC curves are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values, indicating consideration of uncertainty.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention any calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT, which can be considered multi-parametric imaging. There is no comparison with non-contrast CT or justification for using contrast enhancement.

Item24.reason: The study compares the combined model with clinical and radiomics models, which can be considered a comparison with non-radiomic approaches. The study uses the Delong test to compare AUCs.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the models with a simple baseline reference model such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent testing set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study states that the datasets are not publicly available due to data sharing not obtaining the informed consent of the patients.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention sharing any scripts related to automatic segmentation or modeling.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention sharing the final model in any form.
